value.Name: Engine Assembly Specification
value.Description: Master specification document for the turbofan engine assembly. Defines design requirements, performance targets, interface definitions, and manufacturing tolerances.
value.Author: john.smith@company.com

PrimaryContent.TextContent:
Engine Assembly Specification Rev B

1. SCOPE
This specification defines the design and performance requirements for the CF-500 turbofan engine assembly. The engine is designed to produce 22,000 lbf thrust for commercial narrow-body aircraft applications.

2. PERFORMANCE REQUIREMENTS
2.1 Thrust: 22,000 lbf (sea level static, ISA conditions)
2.2 Bypass Ratio: 5.5:1
2.3 Overall Pressure Ratio: 45:1
2.4 Turbine Entry Temperature: 1,650°C maximum
2.5 Specific Fuel Consumption: 0.57 lb/hr/lbf (cruise conditions)
2.6 Dry Weight: 5,500 kg maximum

3. INTERFACE REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Engine mount points conform to SAE AS1222 standard
3.2 Fuel system interface: MIL-DTL-5624 JP-4 compatible
3.3 Electrical interface: 28V DC and 115V 400Hz AC per MIL-STD-704F
3.4 Bleed air extraction: Stage 8 and Stage 14 compressor ports

4. MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Hot section components: Nickel superalloys (Inconel 718, Hastelloy X)
4.2 Cold section rotating parts: Titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)
4.3 Fan blades: Carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP)
4.4 All materials to comply with REACH and RoHS regulations

5. MANUFACTURING TOLERANCES
5.1 Blade tip clearances: 0.25-0.45 mm
5.2 Bearing bore tolerances: IT6 per ISO 286
5.3 Shaft roundness: 0.005 mm max

6. TESTING REQUIREMENTS
6.1 Engine acceptance test per MIL-E-5007
6.2 Endurance testing: 2,000 hours minimum
6.3 Bird strike compliance: FAA AC 33.76

value.Name: Engine Maintenance Manual - Line Maintenance
value.Description: Line maintenance procedures for the CF-500 turbofan engine. Covers scheduled maintenance tasks, inspections, and replacement procedures at transit and overnight stops.
value.Author: emily.zhang@company.com

PrimaryContent.TextContent:
Engine Maintenance Manual - Line Maintenance Rev C

CHAPTER 1 - GENERAL INFORMATION
1.1 Purpose
This manual provides instructions for line maintenance of the CF-500 turbofan engine. All tasks in this manual may be performed at the gate or hangar without engine removal.

1.2 Safety Precautions
WARNING: Ensure engine is shut down and fuel shutoff valve is closed before performing any maintenance.
CAUTION: Do not rotate fan blades by hand. Use only approved turning equipment.

CHAPTER 2 - SCHEDULED MAINTENANCE
2.1 Transit Check (Every flight)
- Visual inspection of engine inlet for FOD
- Check oil level (quantity must be 7-10 quarts)
- Inspect fan blades for nicks and damage per AMM 72-21-01
- Check for fluid leaks (oil, fuel, hydraulic)

2.2 Overnight Check (Daily)
- All transit check items
- Borescope inspection of first stage fan blades
- Drain water from fuel system
- Check bleed air ducting for security
- Inspect thrust reverser for cracks and delamination

2.3 Weekly Check
- All overnight check items
- Replace oil filter if delta-P indicator is activated
- Inspect engine mounts for wear and corrosion
- Check FADEC built-in test (BIT) for fault codes

CHAPTER 3 - COMPONENT REPLACEMENT
3.1 Oil Filter Replacement
Interval: 500 flight hours or when delta-P indicator pops
Procedure:
1. Shut down engine and wait 30 minutes for cooling
2. Place drain pan under oil filter housing
3. Remove filter housing cap (torque: 35 Nm)
4. Remove and discard filter element (Part No. OIL-FILTER-CF500)
5. Install new filter - verify O-ring is seated
6. Torque cap to 35 Nm
7. Run engine and check for leaks

3.2 Igniter Plug Replacement
Interval: 3,000 flight hours
Part Numbers: IGN-PLUG-L (left) IGN-PLUG-R (right)
Procedure:
1. Discharge igniter capacitor using shorting tool
2. Disconnect igniter lead (anti-vibration lockwire)
3. Remove igniter plug with socket P/N 97A3456
4. Gap new plug to 1.5-1.8 mm
5. Install and torque to 20 Nm, safety wire

CHAPTER 4 - FAULT ISOLATION
4.1 High EGT at Take-off
Probable causes:
- Contaminated fuel nozzles - clean or replace
- Compressor stall - check variable geometry schedule
- Turbine blade loss - borescope inspection required
- Bleed air leak - inspect stage 8 and 14 ports

value.Name: Material Certification - Titanium Alloy Ti-6Al-4V
value.Description: Material certification and test reports for titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V used in engine frame and compressor disc manufacturing.
value.Author: raj.mehta@company.com

PrimaryContent.TextContent:
Material Certification Report
Material: Titanium Alloy Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)
Batch Number: TI-2024-A-0047
Supplier: Timet Corporation
Specification: AMS 4928 / AMS 4965

CHEMICAL COMPOSITION (Weight %)
Titanium: Balance
Aluminum: 6.04
Vanadium: 4.12
Iron: 0.18 (max 0.30)
Oxygen: 0.17 (max 0.20)
Nitrogen: 0.008 (max 0.05)
Hydrogen: 0.003 (max 0.015)
Carbon: 0.006 (max 0.10)
Other elements, each: <0.10
Other elements, total: <0.40

MECHANICAL PROPERTIES (Room Temperature)
Ultimate Tensile Strength: 1,000 MPa (min: 897 MPa) - PASS
Yield Strength (0.2%): 896 MPa (min: 828 MPa) - PASS
Elongation: 14.5% (min: 10%) - PASS
Reduction in Area: 38.2% (min: 25%) - PASS
Hardness: 33 HRC (max: 36 HRC) - PASS

MICROSTRUCTURE
Condition: Solution Treated and Aged (STA)
Grain Size: ASTM 7 (fine equiaxed alpha)
Alpha-Beta microstructure confirmed - acceptable

NON-DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
Ultrasonic Inspection: Per AMS 2630, Class A - PASS
Dye Penetrant Inspection: Per AMS 2647 - PASS
No defects found

CERTIFICATION
This material meets the requirements of AMS 4928 Rev S and AMS 4965 Rev M.
Approved for use in: ENG-FRAME-001, ENG-SHAFT-002 (via AMS 4965)
Expiry: Material properties valid for 2 years from test date
Test Date: 2024-05-15
Certified by: Quality Assurance Manager, Timet Corporation

value.Name: Failure Analysis Report - Bearing Spall 2024-09
value.Description: Failure analysis report for main shaft bearing assembly ENG-BEARING-005 that experienced early spallation during accelerated life testing. Root cause identified as lubricant oil starvation.
value.Author: james.wilson@company.com

PrimaryContent.TextContent:
Failure Analysis Report
Document Number: DOC-FAIL-REPORT-004
Part Affected: ENG-BEARING-005 (Main Shaft Bearing Assembly)
Failure Date: 2024-09-03
Test Hours at Failure: 4,200 hours (design life 20,000 hours)

1. DESCRIPTION OF FAILURE
During accelerated life testing at Rig Test Cell 3, the main shaft bearing exhibited spallation on the inner raceway. Surface pitting was observed on 6 of 26 rolling elements. Test was halted at 4,200 equivalent service hours when vibration levels exceeded alarm threshold of 15 mm/s RMS.

2. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
2.1 Visual Inspection
- Spalling on inner raceway: 15mm diameter area at 6 o'clock position
- Bearing balls: surface pitting on 6 balls (Grade 3 damage per ABMA standard)
- Cage: intact, no deformation
- Outer raceway: no damage observed

2.2 SEM Analysis
Scanning electron microscopy showed:
- Fatigue crack initiation at subsurface carbide inclusion
- Crack propagation in rolling contact direction
- Final fracture mode: ductile tearing

2.3 Oil Analysis
Oil samples taken at 100, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 hours:
- At 4,000 hours: iron particles = 45 ppm (alarm limit: 25 ppm)
- At 4,200 hours (failure): iron particles = 120 ppm
- Conclusion: progressive bearing wear prior to catastrophic spall

3. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS (5-Why)
Why 1: Spallation occurred → Contact stress exceeded material fatigue limit
Why 2: High contact stress → Insufficient oil film between ball and raceway
Why 3: Thin oil film → Lubricant starvation at bearing
Why 4: Lubricant starvation → Oil feed jet diameter undersized (0.5 mm actual vs. 0.9 mm design)
Why 5: Wrong jet size → Drawing error on Component Drawing DWG-BEAR-005-003 Rev A
Root Cause: Drafting error resulted in undersized lubrication jet orifice

4. CORRECTIVE ACTIONS
4.1 Immediate: Quarantine all ENG-BEARING-005 assemblies manufactured with Rev A drawing
4.2 Short-term: Issue Engineering Change Notice ECN-2024-0047 to correct jet orifice to 0.9 mm
4.3 Long-term: Add lubrication jet inspection to acceptance test procedure

5. RECOMMENDATIONS
- Revise part drawing DWG-BEAR-005-003 to Rev B with correct orifice size
- Update QC inspection to include jet orifice diameter verification
- Re-test redesigned bearing to confirm life target of 20,000 hours

value.Name: FADEC System Requirements Specification
value.Description: Software requirements specification for the Full Authority Digital Engine Controller (FADEC) system. Defines functional, performance, and safety requirements per DO-178C Level A.
value.Author: raj.mehta@company.com

PrimaryContent.TextContent:
FADEC System Requirements Specification Rev C
Document: DOC-FADEC-SRS-005
Development Assurance Level: DO-178C Level A

1. SYSTEM OVERVIEW
The CF-500 FADEC is a dual-channel digital control system that provides complete authority over engine operation. The system eliminates mechanical hydromechanical units and provides precise fuel control, variable geometry scheduling, and health monitoring.

2. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
2.1 Fuel Control
REQ-FC-001: System shall compute fuel flow command within 10 ms of power lever angle change
REQ-FC-002: System shall maintain N1 speed within ±0.5% of demanded speed
REQ-FC-003: System shall limit EGT to 960°C (takeoff limit) with ±5°C accuracy
REQ-FC-004: System shall provide automatic engine acceleration from idle to max in <5 seconds

2.2 Variable Geometry Control
REQ-VG-001: System shall schedule variable inlet guide vanes (VIGVs) as a function of N1 and ambient conditions
REQ-VG-002: System shall detect VIGV position error >2° and trigger fault isolation
REQ-VG-003: Variable bleed valves shall be controlled to prevent compressor stall

2.3 Engine Limiting
REQ-LIM-001: System shall prevent N1 overspeed above 104% N1 rated speed
REQ-LIM-002: System shall prevent N2 overspeed above 105% N2 rated speed
REQ-LIM-003: Oil temperature limit shall be enforced at 155°C with 0°C hysteresis
REQ-LIM-004: Oil pressure low limit shall be 40 PSI with 5 PSI hysteresis

3. PERFORMANCE REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Processing
REQ-PERF-001: Control loop execution rate: 80 Hz (12.5 ms period)
REQ-PERF-002: Sensor reading latency: <2 ms from sensor input to control algorithm
REQ-PERF-003: ARINC 429 output refresh rate: 50 Hz for primary parameters

3.2 Reliability
REQ-REL-001: System probability of loss of thrust control: <1×10⁻⁹ per flight hour
REQ-REL-002: Dual-channel architecture shall provide continued operation with single channel failure
REQ-REL-003: MTBF for electronic hardware: minimum 20,000 hours

4. SAFETY REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Hazard Classification
Engine in-flight shutdown: Hazardous per ARP4761
Loss of thrust control: Catastrophic per ARP4761

4.2 Architecture
SEQ-ARCH-001: Dual redundant channels with cross-channel data link
SEQ-ARCH-002: Dissimilar hardware in each channel (different ASIC suppliers)
SEQ-ARCH-003: Independent power supplies for each channel

5. INTERFACE REQUIREMENTS
5.1 Cockpit Interface
- Thrust Lever Angle (TLA) input: ARINC 429
- Engine display output: ARINC 429 labels 100-199
- Maintenance data: ACARS via ARINC 429

5.2 Engine Sensor Inputs
- N1 speed: dual magnetic pick-ups
- N2 speed: dual magnetic pick-ups
- EGT: 8× Type K thermocouples, averaged
- Fuel flow: dual turbine flow meters
- Oil pressure: 2× piezoelectric pressure transducers
